entries.title: *De Lapide Philosophico* — First Figure: the two fish in the sea
entries.creator: 'Lambspring' (engraver anonymous; text ed. Nicolas Barnaud / Lucas Jennis)
entries.date: 1625 (engraving; first issued 1599, here in the Musaeum Hermeticum)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — Internet Archive scan of the Musaeum Hermeticum (Frankfurt, 1625)]
entries.rights: Public domain. From the Musaeum Hermeticum. Via Internet Archive.
entries.summary:
The opening plate of the celebrated *De Lapide Philosophico* of 'Lambspring' — the **Prima Figura**, two great fish swimming in a calm sea. The Latin verse above reads *Pisces duos in nostro mari natare* ("two fishes swim in our sea"), and the motto below glosses it plainly: *Mare est Corpus, duo Pisces sunt Spiritus & Anima* — "the Sea is the Body, the two Fishes are Spirit and Soul."

## Iconography
Across a wide bay two large fish glide just beneath the surface, while in the middle distance a sailing-boat and ships ride at anchor before a walled coastal town backed by hills. The engraving is small, square, and set into a letterpress page with its figure-number and printer's signatures showing.

## Significance
The two fish are the foundational pair of the whole sequence: the volatile and the fixed, [[motif:soul|Soul]] and [[motif:spirit|Spirit]], held within the one watery [[motif:prima-materia|Body]] of the Work. The 'Lambspring' tract — a German verse cycle ascribed to a half-legendary author, latinized by Nicolas Barnaud and given these fifteen plates — was among the most reprinted of all alchemical emblem books, gathered into the *Musaeum Hermeticum* of Lucas Jennis. The motif of two fishes in one sea recurs across the tradition as a sign of the dual principles that must be reconciled.

## For artists & game designers
A serene establishing shot — calm bay, ships, twin fish below the waterline — ideal as a title card or the first beat of an alchemical quest where two principles must later be joined.

citations.text: 'Lambspring', *De Lapide Philosophico*, in *Musaeum Hermeticum Reformatum et Amplificatum* (Frankfurt: Lucas Jennis, 1625).
Adam McLean (ed.), *The Book of Lambspring* (Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
Internet Archive

entries.title: *De Lapide Philosophico* — Second Figure: the knight and the dragon (Putrefactio)
entries.creator: 'Lambspring' (engraver anonymous; text ed. Nicolas Barnaud / Lucas Jennis)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — Internet Archive scan of the Musaeum Hermeticum (Frankfurt, 1625)]
entries.rights: Public domain. From the Musaeum Hermeticum. Via Internet Archive.
entries.summary:
The **Secunda Figura** of 'Lambspring': an armoured knight confronts a winged dragon at the edge of a forest. The verse warns *Hic celeriter animadvertite / Nigram feram in sylva* ("here swiftly mark the black beast in the wood"); the single word beneath the cut names the operation — **PUTREFACTIO**.

## Iconography
A man-at-arms in plate armour, sword raised and round shield advanced, lunges at a scaly winged dragon that rears among rocks and trees, a lake and hilltop town beyond. The dragon is the *fera nigra*, the black beast lurking in the green wood of the matter.

## Significance
The dragon here is the [[motif:prima-materia|prime matter]] in its raw, poisonous, undifferentiated state, and the knight's assault is the violence of [[motif:putrefaction|putrefaction]] — the blackening (*nigredo*) by which the old body is broken down so that something new may arise. In the 'Lambspring' grammar the forest is the secret vessel and the dragon the matter that must first be slain before it can be exalted.

## For artists & game designers
A ready-made boss encounter: armoured hero versus a forest dragon, with the alchemical subtext that the 'death' here is a necessary stage of transformation, not a final defeat.

citations.text: 'Lambspring', *De Lapide Philosophico*, in *Musaeum Hermeticum Reformatum et Amplificatum* (Frankfurt: Lucas Jennis, 1625).
Adam McLean (ed.), *The Book of Lambspring* (Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
Internet Archive

entries.title: *De Lapide Philosophico* — Third Figure: the stag and the unicorn in the forest
entries.creator: 'Lambspring' (engraver anonymous; text ed. Nicolas Barnaud / Lucas Jennis)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — Internet Archive scan of the Musaeum Hermeticum (Frankfurt, 1625)]
entries.rights: Public domain. From the Musaeum Hermeticum. Via Internet Archive.
entries.summary:
The **Tertia Figura** — one of the best-loved of all alchemical emblems — showing a stag and a unicorn meeting in a wood. The verse instructs *In sylva cervum & unicornu latere* ("in the forest lie hidden a stag and a unicorn"), and the motto below declares *In Corpore est Anima & Spiritus* — "in the Body are Soul and Spirit."

## Iconography
In a clearing fringed with trees an antlered stag stands at left and a long-horned unicorn at right, facing one another across rocky ground, with a glimpse of a pool and distant building behind. The two beasts are emblems of the same hidden pair the fish announced.

## Significance
The stag is the [[motif:soul|Soul]] and the unicorn the [[motif:spirit|Spirit]] (or, in other readings, the reverse) — the volatile powers concealed within the body of the matter, the forest again standing for the vessel. This figure is the source of countless later borrowings; it became a near-heraldic shorthand for the alchemical conjunction of opposites and circulates widely in modern occult and design contexts.

## For artists & game designers
Iconic heraldic pairing — stag and unicorn confronting in a glade — directly usable as an emblem, sigil, faction crest, or the visual key to a 'reconcile the two beasts' puzzle.

citations.text: 'Lambspring', *De Lapide Philosophico*, in *Musaeum Hermeticum Reformatum et Amplificatum* (Frankfurt: Lucas Jennis, 1625).
Adam McLean (ed.), *The Book of Lambspring* (Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
Internet Archive

entries.title: *De Lapide Philosophico* — Third Figure (detail): stag and unicorn
entries.creator: 'Lambspring' (engraver anonymous; text ed. Nicolas Barnaud / Lucas Jennis)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — cropped detail of the Tertia Figura; Wikimedia/Internet Archive derivative]
entries.rights: Public domain. From the Musaeum Hermeticum. Via Internet Archive.
entries.summary:
A tightly cropped detail of the famous **Third Figure** of 'Lambspring', isolating just the stag and the unicorn within the engraved frame, stripped of the surrounding letterpress. It is the same scene whose motto runs *In Corpore est Anima & Spiritus* — "in the Body are Soul and Spirit."

## Iconography
The close crop fills the field with the two animals on their rocky shelf among trees: the antlered stag at left, the spiral-horned unicorn at right, a wooded landscape behind. Without the Latin verse the image reads purely as a heraldic confrontation of beasts.

## Significance
This is the most reproduced single image from the whole 'Lambspring' series, and such detail-crops are how it most often travels — as a self-contained emblem of [[motif:soul|Soul]] and [[motif:spirit|Spirit]] hidden in the body of the matter, the two volatile principles awaiting their union. Its clean silhouette has made it a favourite source for bookplates, tattoos, and occult design.

## For artists & game designers
The cleanest possible reference for the stag-and-unicorn motif — high-contrast, frameless, ready to drop into an emblem, loading screen, or crest.

citations.text: 'Lambspring', *De Lapide Philosophico*, in *Musaeum Hermeticum Reformatum et Amplificatum* (Frankfurt: Lucas Jennis, 1625).
Adam McLean (ed.), *The Book of Lambspring* (Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
Internet Archive

entries.title: *De Lapide Philosophico* — Fourth Figure: the two lions become one
entries.creator: 'Lambspring' (engraver anonymous; text ed. Nicolas Barnaud / Lucas Jennis)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — Internet Archive scan of the Musaeum Hermeticum (Frankfurt, 1625)]
entries.rights: Public domain. From the Musaeum Hermeticum. Via Internet Archive.
entries.summary:
The **Quarta Figura**, in which two lions stand together in a forest clearing, their tails seeming to merge into a single intertwined coil. The verse exclaims *Est summum portentum / Ex duobus leonibus unum fieri* ("it is the greatest marvel that from two lions one is made"), and the motto below: *Spiritus & Anima sunt conjungendi & redigendi ad corpus suum* — "Spirit and Soul are to be joined and brought back to their body."

## Iconography
Two lions — one often read as male and one female — pace side by side between framing trees, a wooded landscape behind, their serpentine tails twining toward one. The doubling-into-one is the visual argument of the plate.

## Significance
The two lions restate the central drama of the Work: the [[motif:soul|Soul]] and [[motif:spirit|Spirit]] (the fixed and the volatile) must be reunited and led back into the [[motif:prima-materia|body]] from which they were drawn. The lion is a classic alchemical sign of a fierce, devouring agent — here doubled and reconciled, prefiguring the kingly conjunctions of the later plates.

## For artists & game designers
Twin-lion heraldry with a built-in 'fusion' motif — excellent for a sigil, a paired-boss design, or an emblem signalling the merging of two powers into one.

citations.text: 'Lambspring', *De Lapide Philosophico*, in *Musaeum Hermeticum Reformatum et Amplificatum* (Frankfurt: Lucas Jennis, 1625).
Adam McLean (ed.), *The Book of Lambspring* (Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
Internet Archive

entries.title: *De Lapide Philosophico* — Fifth Figure: the wolf and the dog
entries.creator: 'Lambspring' (engraver anonymous; text ed. Nicolas Barnaud / Lucas Jennis)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — Internet Archive scan of the Musaeum Hermeticum (Frankfurt, 1625)]
entries.rights: Public domain. From the Musaeum Hermeticum. Via Internet Archive.
entries.summary:
The **Quinta Figura**, the wolf and the dog locked in combat before a river town. The verse runs *Lupus & Canis sunt in una domo / Postremo tamen ex his unum fit* ("a wolf and a dog are in one house, yet at last from these one is made"); the motto below names the work: *Mortificatio, & albificatio, corporis conjunctique cum Anima & Spiritu imbibitio* — "the mortification and whitening, and the imbibing of the conjoined body with Soul and Spirit."

## Iconography
Under a tree at the bank of a wide river two beasts grapple — a lean wolf and a domestic dog rearing against each other — with a walled town and church on the far shore. They share, as the verse says, one house.

## Significance
The wolf is the volatile, untamed principle and the dog the fixed, domesticated one — antimony and the metal, or the fierce and the gentle mercury — set at strife so that out of their mortal struggle one purified substance ('whitened', *albificatio*) may emerge. This pairing of grey wolf and dog is one of the most quoted images in later alchemical literature, taken up notably in the Mylius and Ripley traditions.

## For artists & game designers
A dynamic two-animal clash with a clear meaning — opposed natures fighting toward a single outcome; useful for a transformation cutscene or a 'tame the beast' mechanic.

citations.text: 'Lambspring', *De Lapide Philosophico*, in *Musaeum Hermeticum Reformatum et Amplificatum* (Frankfurt: Lucas Jennis, 1625).
Adam McLean (ed.), *The Book of Lambspring* (Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
Internet Archive

entries.title: *De Lapide Philosophico* — Sixth Figure: the dragon devouring its tail
entries.creator: 'Lambspring' (engraver anonymous; text ed. Nicolas Barnaud / Lucas Jennis)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — Internet Archive scan of the Musaeum Hermeticum (Frankfurt, 1625)]
entries.rights: Public domain. From the Musaeum Hermeticum. Via Internet Archive.
entries.summary:
The **Sexta Figura**: a great winged dragon coiled in a forest, biting at its own tail — the ouroboros. The verse declares *Hoc vere est magnum miraculum & cita fraus, / In venenoso Dracone summam medicinam inesse* ("this is truly a great marvel and a swift deceit, that in the venomous dragon lies the highest medicine"); the gloss below: *Mercurius recte & chymice praecipitatus vel sublimatus, in sua propria Aqua resolutus & rursum coagulatus* — mercury rightly precipitated or sublimed, dissolved in its own water and again coagulated.

## Iconography
A scaly, bat-winged dragon turns its long neck back upon itself amid trees and underbrush, the forest opening to hills beyond. The serpent-circle is the self-consuming, self-renewing matter.

## Significance
The poisonous dragon that nonetheless holds the *summa medicina* is the philosophical [[motif:mercury|Mercurius]] — the agent that destroys and heals, that must be 'dissolved in its own water and again coagulated'. The [[motif:ouroboros|ouroboros]] form makes explicit the circular *solve et coagula* at the heart of the Work: nothing is added from outside; the matter perfects itself by turning upon itself.

## For artists & game designers
A forest ouroboros — the canonical 'venom that is also medicine' symbol; perfect for a cyclical mechanic, a respawn glyph, or an item that harms and heals at once.

citations.text: 'Lambspring', *De Lapide Philosophico*, in *Musaeum Hermeticum Reformatum et Amplificatum* (Frankfurt: Lucas Jennis, 1625).
Adam McLean (ed.), *The Book of Lambspring* (Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
Internet Archive

entries.title: *De Lapide Philosophico* — Sixth Figure (detail): the ouroboros dragon
entries.creator: 'Lambspring' (engraver anonymous; text ed. Nicolas Barnaud / Lucas Jennis)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — cropped detail of the Sexta Figura; Wikimedia/Internet Archive derivative]
entries.rights: Public domain. From the Musaeum Hermeticum. Via Internet Archive.
entries.summary:
A close crop of the **Sixth Figure** dragon, isolating the winged ouroboros within its forest setting and dropping the letterpress verse. It is the same self-devouring serpent the text names the 'venomous dragon' in which 'the highest medicine' is hidden.

## Iconography
The bat-winged, scaly dragon curls into a near-circle on the woodland floor, jaws turned to its tail, trees rising behind. The crop emphasises the closed loop of the body — the ouroboros read as pure form.

## Significance
Detail-crops like this are how the 'Lambspring' dragon most often circulates as a standalone [[motif:ouroboros|ouroboros]] emblem — the figure of cyclical [[motif:mercury|Mercurius]], the matter that perfects itself through endless dissolution and coagulation. It is one of the most recognisable serpent-circles in the whole engraved corpus of alchemy.

## For artists & game designers
A frameless forest ouroboros, high contrast and self-contained — drop-in art for a cyclical glyph, an infinity sigil, or a save/respawn icon.

citations.text: 'Lambspring', *De Lapide Philosophico*, in *Musaeum Hermeticum Reformatum et Amplificatum* (Frankfurt: Lucas Jennis, 1625).
Adam McLean (ed.), *The Book of Lambspring* (Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
Internet Archive

entries.title: *De Lapide Philosophico* — Seventh Figure: the two birds in the forest
entries.creator: 'Lambspring' (engraver anonymous; text ed. Nicolas Barnaud / Lucas Jennis)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — Internet Archive scan of the Musaeum Hermeticum (Frankfurt, 1625)]
entries.rights: Public domain. From the Musaeum Hermeticum. Via Internet Archive.
entries.summary:
The **Septima Figura**: two birds in a great forest tree, one rising in flight and one perched. The verse reads *Duae aves in sylva nominantur, / Cum tamen saltem una intelligatur* ("two birds are named in the wood, though only one is to be understood"); the gloss: *Mercurius saepius sublimatus, tandem figitur, ut non amplius aufugere & avolare per vim ignis possit; Toties enim sublimatio reiteranda, quousque fixus fiat* — mercury, often sublimed, is at last fixed so that it can no longer fly off by force of fire.

## Iconography
A tall oak fills the right of the frame; one bird beats upward through its branches while a second sits below, and a snail creeps across a fallen log in the foreground — the slow, fixed against the swift, volatile. A wooded valley with a church recedes at left.

## Significance
The two birds are one and the same [[motif:mercury|Mercurius]] in two states — volatile (flying) and fixed (perched) — the verse insisting that though *two* are named, only *one* substance is meant. The figure teaches the repeated sublimation by which the volatile spirit is at last 'fixed' so it can no longer escape the fire. The earth-bound snail is a quiet emblem of that final fixity.

## For artists & game designers
A neat 'one thing, two states' visual — bird in flight versus bird at rest, with a snail for slowness; useful where a single resource toggles between volatile and stable forms.

citations.text: 'Lambspring', *De Lapide Philosophico*, in *Musaeum Hermeticum Reformatum et Amplificatum* (Frankfurt: Lucas Jennis, 1625).
Adam McLean (ed.), *The Book of Lambspring* (Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
Internet Archive

entries.title: *De Lapide Philosophico* — Eighth Figure: the bird of Hermes and its mate
entries.creator: 'Lambspring' (engraver anonymous; text ed. Nicolas Barnaud / Lucas Jennis)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — Internet Archive scan of the Musaeum Hermeticum (Frankfurt, 1625)]
entries.rights: Public domain. From the Musaeum Hermeticum. Via Internet Archive.
entries.summary:
The **Octava Figura**, the so-called bird of Hermes: a large winged bird in a forest stoops over a smaller one. The verse names them *Duae aves sunt nobiles & magni pretii, / Corpus & Spiritus, alterum consumit* ("two birds are noble and of great price, Body and Spirit, the one consumes the other"). The long gloss directs that the body be set again 'for digestion' in horse-dung or a bath, the white work done and the red prepared by art — *praeparaturque sic Lapis Philosophorum*.

## Iconography
Beneath a broad tree a great spread-winged bird bends toward a lesser bird on the ground, a coastal landscape with sailing ships beyond. One devours the other — the larger nature absorbing the smaller.

## Significance
This is the 'bird of Hermes' motif familiar from the Ripley *Cantilena* — 'eating his wings to make him tame' — here the [[motif:spirit|Spirit]] and [[motif:prima-materia|Body]] in which one consumes the other so that a single, more perfect nature results. The accompanying text explicitly carries the Work toward the white and then the red, naming the goal as the Philosophers' Stone itself.

## For artists & game designers
A dramatic predation image with a redemptive meaning — the greater bird absorbing the lesser to become whole; strong material for an absorption or fusion mechanic.

citations.text: 'Lambspring', *De Lapide Philosophico*, in *Musaeum Hermeticum Reformatum et Amplificatum* (Frankfurt: Lucas Jennis, 1625).
Adam McLean (ed.), *The Book of Lambspring* (Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
Internet Archive

entries.title: *De Lapide Philosophico* — Ninth Figure: the King attains his kingdom
entries.creator: 'Lambspring' (engraver anonymous; text ed. Nicolas Barnaud / Lucas Jennis)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — Internet Archive scan of the Musaeum Hermeticum (Frankfurt, 1625)]
entries.rights: Public domain. From the Musaeum Hermeticum. Via Internet Archive.
entries.summary:
The **Nona Figura**: a crowned King enthroned upon an arched bridge or pavilion, holding the orb-and-cross. The verse proclaims *Dominus sylvarum potitus est suo regno, / Et ab infimo ad supremum gradum conscendit* ("the Lord of the forests has gained his kingdom, and has climbed from the lowest to the highest degree"). The closing line, *Intellige primum Gradum Tincturae vere apparuisse*, declares that the first true degree of the Tincture has now appeared.

## Iconography
The King sits robed and crowned in a canopied seat raised on a masonry bridge above a river and walled town; he bears the globus cruciger of sovereignty. The whole rises 'from the lowest to the highest', the architecture echoing the ascent named in the verse.

## Significance
With this plate the bestial and avian transformations give way to the human kingly imagery of the later figures: the matter, having passed through death and reconciliation, is exalted to royalty — the first appearance of the [[motif:tincture|Tincture]]. The forest-creatures are mastered ('Lord of the forests'), and the Work crowns its perfected substance as a King.

## For artists & game designers
A coronation/ascension beat — enthroned monarch with orb on a bridge over a town; ideal as a mid-game 'the work bears fruit' tableau or a level-up throne scene.

citations.text: 'Lambspring', *De Lapide Philosophico*, in *Musaeum Hermeticum Reformatum et Amplificatum* (Frankfurt: Lucas Jennis, 1625).
Adam McLean (ed.), *The Book of Lambspring* (Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
Internet Archive

entries.title: *De Lapide Philosophico* — Tenth Figure: the salamander in the fire
entries.creator: 'Lambspring' (engraver anonymous; text ed. Nicolas Barnaud / Lucas Jennis)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — Internet Archive scan of the Musaeum Hermeticum (Frankfurt, 1625)]
entries.rights: Public domain. From the Musaeum Hermeticum. Via Internet Archive.
entries.summary:
The **Decima Figura**: a salamander lying unharmed in a blaze while a near-naked man rakes the fire. The verse runs *Salamandra vivit in igne, / Ignisque hanc mutavit in optimum colorem* ("the salamander lives in the fire, and the fire has changed it to the best colour"); the gloss reads *Reiteratio, gradatio & melioratio Tincturae, vel Lapidis Philosophorum: Augmentatio potius intelligatur* — the reiteration, gradation and bettering of the Tincture or Stone; understand rather its augmentation.

## Iconography
Flames and rolling smoke fill the left of the cut; the spotted salamander rests within the fire while a labourer, loin-clad, leans in with a long rake to tend the blaze. Hills and a tower close the background.

## Significance
The salamander — the creature fabled to live in and feed on fire — is the perfected [[motif:tincture|Tincture]] that not only survives the flame but is improved by it. This plate marks the stage of [[motif:multiplication|augmentation and multiplication]]: the Stone, once made, is strengthened and increased by repeated firing, 'changed to the best colour'.

## For artists & game designers
A fire-dwelling salamander tended by a stoker — a vivid emblem of an item that grows stronger when burned; ready material for a forge-upgrade or fire-immunity icon.

citations.text: 'Lambspring', *De Lapide Philosophico*, in *Musaeum Hermeticum Reformatum et Amplificatum* (Frankfurt: Lucas Jennis, 1625).
Adam McLean (ed.), *The Book of Lambspring* (Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
Internet Archive

entries.title: *De Lapide Philosophico* — Eleventh Figure: Father, Son and the grey Guide
entries.creator: 'Lambspring' (engraver anonymous; text ed. Nicolas Barnaud / Lucas Jennis)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — Internet Archive scan of the Musaeum Hermeticum (Frankfurt, 1625)]
entries.rights: Public domain. From the Musaeum Hermeticum. Via Internet Archive.
entries.summary:
The **Undecima Figura**: three figures walk hand in hand by a shore — an aged, long-bearded Father, a young crowned Son in armour, and a winged grey-robed Guide. The verse reads *Pater, Filius, cum ductore sibi sunt juncti manibus* ("Father, Son, with the guide, are joined to one another by the hands"), glossed *Corpus, Spiritus & Anima, hic subintelligitur* — "here Body, Spirit and Soul are to be understood."

## Iconography
The grey-bearded Father leads at left with a sceptre, the youthful Son between, and the winged Guide (the *ductor*) at right; behind them a castle, the sea with a ship, and a craggy mountain. The three are linked by their joined hands.

## Significance
The human triad makes literal the inner cast of the whole Work: [[motif:prima-materia|Body]] (Father), [[motif:spirit|Spirit]] (Son) and [[motif:soul|Soul]], led by the grey-cloaked Guide — the philosophical Mercurius or inner teacher who conducts the substances through their stages. This figure opens the closing 'family drama' of the series: father and son guided toward death, ascent and reunion.

## For artists & game designers
A three-character party led by a hooded mentor — an archetypal escort/journey grouping with built-in symbolic roles (body, spirit, soul) for a guided-quest sequence.

citations.text: 'Lambspring', *De Lapide Philosophico*, in *Musaeum Hermeticum Reformatum et Amplificatum* (Frankfurt: Lucas Jennis, 1625).
Adam McLean (ed.), *The Book of Lambspring* (Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
Internet Archive

entries.title: *De Lapide Philosophico* — Twelfth Figure: the ascent to the mountain-top
entries.creator: 'Lambspring' (engraver anonymous; text ed. Nicolas Barnaud / Lucas Jennis)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — Internet Archive scan of the Musaeum Hermeticum (Frankfurt, 1625)]
entries.rights: Public domain. From the Musaeum Hermeticum. Via Internet Archive.
entries.summary:
The **Duodecima Figura**: the winged Guide and the crowned Son stand together on a high mountain peak beneath a sun, a crescent moon and a field of stars. The verse reads *Alius mons Indiae in vase jacet, / Quem Spiritus & Anima, utpote filius & dux, conscenderunt* ("another mountain of India lies in the vessel, which Spirit and Soul, namely the son and the guide, have climbed").

## Iconography
From a soaring crag the two figures gesture upward; the radiant sun blazes at left, the moon hangs at right amid stars, and a vast landscape of bays, towns and ships spreads far below. The 'mountain of India' is, the verse says, contained *in vase* — within the alchemical vessel.

## Significance
The ascent figures the sublimation and exaltation of the volatile principles: [[motif:spirit|Spirit]] (the Son) and [[motif:soul|Soul]] (or, with the Guide, the conducting Mercurius) rise to the summit. The pointed reminder that the whole cosmic mountain lies 'in the vessel' is the tract's insistence that this grand spiritual ascent is enacted, materially, inside the sealed flask.

## For artists & game designers
A summit-attainment vista — two figures on a peak under sun, moon and stars over a whole world below; an excellent 'ascension' or chapter-climax establishing shot.

citations.text: 'Lambspring', *De Lapide Philosophico*, in *Musaeum Hermeticum Reformatum et Amplificatum* (Frankfurt: Lucas Jennis, 1625).
Adam McLean (ed.), *The Book of Lambspring* (Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
Internet Archive

entries.title: *De Lapide Philosophico* — Thirteenth Figure: the King devours his Son
entries.creator: 'Lambspring' (engraver anonymous; text ed. Nicolas Barnaud / Lucas Jennis)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — Internet Archive scan of the Musaeum Hermeticum (Frankfurt, 1625)]
entries.rights: Public domain. From the Musaeum Hermeticum. Via Internet Archive.
entries.summary:
The **Decimatertia Figura**, one of the most arresting plates of the series: the old crowned King draws his kneeling Son toward his mouth as if to consume him, while the winged Guide stands by, gesturing. The verse states it baldly — *Hic Pater devorat Filium: / Anima & Spiritus e corpore promanant* ("here the Father devours the Son: Soul and Spirit issue forth from the body").

## Iconography
In a curtained, arcaded palace the bearded King sits enthroned on a raised dais and bends to engulf the young King who kneels before him; the grey winged *ductor* presides at left, a townscape framed in the arches. The act is intimate and terrible at once.

## Significance
This cannibal kingship is a famous alchemical topos — the old fixed body reabsorbing the volatile son so that [[motif:soul|Soul]] and [[motif:spirit|Spirit]] may be driven out and the matter reduced once more. It belongs to the same imaginative family as Saturn devouring his children and the 'green lion devouring the sun'; here it is the penultimate death before the sweating cure and the final reunion.

## For artists & game designers
A shocking yet meaningful image — a king consuming his heir as a stage of renewal; potent material for a sacrifice mechanic or a dark transformation cutscene where loss is the path to power.

citations.text: 'Lambspring', *De Lapide Philosophico*, in *Musaeum Hermeticum Reformatum et Amplificatum* (Frankfurt: Lucas Jennis, 1625).
Adam McLean (ed.), *The Book of Lambspring* (Magnum Opus Hermetic Sourceworks).
Internet Archive

entries.title: *De Lapide Philosophico* — Fourteenth Figure: the King sweating in his bed
entries.creator: 'Lambspring' (engraver anonymous; text ed. Nicolas Barnaud / Lucas Jennis)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — Internet Archive scan of the Musaeum Hermeticum (Frankfurt, 1625)]
entries.rights: Public domain. From the Musaeum Hermeticum. Via Internet Archive.
entries.summary:
The **Decimaquarta Figura**: the old King lies in a curtained canopy bed, his crowned head on the pillow, sweating profusely. The verse explains *Hic vehementer sudat Pater, / Oleum & recta Tinctura Philosophorum ex eo promanat* ("here the Father sweats vehemently; the Oil and the true Tincture of the Philosophers flow forth from him").

## Iconography
A richly carved four-poster bed dominates the chamber, drapes drawn back; the bearded King reclines beneath the covers, a pair of slippers and a vessel set on the boards below, daylight at the window. The scene is quiet, domestic, convalescent — the sweat itself the precious product.

## Significance
Having devoured his Son, the King falls sick and undergoes the 'sweating cure': from his fevered body issue the *Oleum* and the true [[motif:tincture|Tincture]] of the Philosophers — the dissolution and exudation that yield the medicine. It is the penultimate plate, the death-and-passion of the old king turning at last to the regenerated life of the final figure.

## For artists & game designers
A convalescence/transmutation-by-sweat scene — sickbed of a king from whom a precious essence is distilled; useful for a healing-ritual beat or an alchemical 'extract the cure' moment.

entries.title: *De Lapide Philosophico* — Fifteenth Figure: Father and Son made one
entries.creator: 'Lambspring' (engraver anonymous; text ed. Nicolas Barnaud / Lucas Jennis)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — Internet Archive scan of the Musaeum Hermeticum (Frankfurt, 1625)]
entries.rights: Public domain. From the Musaeum Hermeticum. Via Internet Archive.
entries.summary:
The **Decimaquinta Figura**, the closing plate: three figures sit enthroned together beneath a draped canopy — a crowned young King at left, the winged grey Guide at centre, and the old crowned Father-King at right. The verse seals the cycle: *Hic Pater & Filius in unum sunt copulati, / Ut simul in aeternum manent* ("here Father and Son are coupled into one, that together they may abide for ever").

## Iconography
The regained Son, restored after his devouring and the King's sweating cure, is enthroned in equal majesty beside the Father, the Guide between them as the bond of their union; an arcaded hall opens behind. The three sit in calm, frontal symmetry — the resolved triad.

## Significance
The final figure is the *coniunctio* perfected: [[motif:prima-materia|Body]], [[motif:spirit|Spirit]] and [[motif:soul|Soul]] reunited and fixed 'into one' for ever — the completed Stone. Father and Son, after death and rebirth, reign together, mediated by the Guide (Mercurius). The whole 'Lambspring' sequence has carried the reader from the two fish in the sea to this eternal, threefold kingship — among the most quoted resolutions in the emblem-literature of alchemy.

## For artists & game designers
A triumphant 'reunion / completion' tableau — restored heir, elder king and mediating guide enthroned together; a natural finale screen or victory tableau for an alchemical narrative.

entries.title: *De Lapide Philosophico* — Preface leaf with the Lambspring arms (rebus crest)
entries.creator: 'Lambspring' (engraver anonymous; text ed. Nicolas Barnaud / Lucas Jennis)
entries.repository: [PLACEHOLDER: per source — Internet Archive scan of the Musaeum Hermeticum (Frankfurt, 1625)]
entries.rights: Public domain. From the Musaeum Hermeticum. Via Internet Archive.
entries.summary:
The **Praefatio** leaf of the 'Lambspring' tract, carrying its small engraved coat-of-arms above the prose. The verse heading reads *Lambsprinck vocor, natus ex nobili familia, / Et hoc stemma gero cum laude & jure* ("I am called Lambspring, born of a noble family, and I bear this crest with praise and by right"). Recorded honestly as a text page with its heraldic device rather than as one of the fifteen Figures.

## Iconography
A shield bearing a ram or sheep is surmounted, in the crest, by a second lamb standing upon scrolling mantling — a visual rebus on the name 'Lambspring' (Lamm + Sprinck, the leaping lamb). Letterpress preface fills the rest of the leaf, with the page-number and signatures showing.

## Significance
The arms are the device of the half-legendary 'Lambspring', the supposed German author whose name the rebus crest puns upon. The tract claims noble descent and learned authority — *Philosophiam legi, & bene intellexi* ('I have read philosophy and understood it well'). The leaf frames the fifteen emblems that follow and anchors the work's persona of an ancient German *philosophus*.

## For artists & game designers
A period rebus coat-of-arms — leaping lamb crest over a ram shield — usable as an authentic heraldic device, faction crest, or decorated front-matter prop.

entries.shelfmark: title page (Francofurti, apud Hermannum à Sande, 1677)
entries.summary:
The engraved title page of a separate Frankfurt edition of the 'Lambspring' tract — *LAMBSPRINCK, Nobilis Germani Philosophi Antiqui Libellus De Lapide Philosophico, e Germanico versu Latine redditus, per Nicolaum Barnaudum Delphinatem Medicum … Francofurti, Apud Hermannum à Sande. MDCLXXVII.* Recorded as a title page rather than one of the fifteen Figures.

## Iconography
Beneath the letterpress title stands a small engraved vignette: a robed, grey-bearded philosopher holding a tall sceptre or staff beside a domed, arch-mouthed furnace or athanor, ruins at left and a comet or star streaking the sky. The figure is the type of the ancient sage-adept presiding over the Work.

## Significance
This leaf documents the bibliographic frame of the famous emblem cycle: a German verse libellus attributed to 'Lambspring', a noble ancient German philosopher, rendered into Latin by the physician Nicolas Barnaud. The same fifteen plates circulated both here and within Lucas Jennis's *Musaeum Hermeticum* — the gathering that carried 'Lambspring' to its lasting fame.

## For artists & game designers
A period title-page vignette — sage with staff beside an athanor under a comet — handy as an authentic book-cover plate, a loading screen, or a 'master alchemist' character reference.
